motto: Serving Greywater Falls since 1887, and the lake somewhat longer.
weather: Overcast and still, the clouds the colour of old pewter pressing down low without quite breaking. Temperature at dawn: 31°F. No wind. A white fog sits on the lake in a single flat layer, ending at the shore as if it were a lid.

undercurrent.note: The fourth letter's arrival closes the loop — it has now addressed an existing, named, un-arrivable resident in Agnes Crewe, the town's oldest and most knowing. Agnes's response ('It knows the address') is the season's most direct acknowledgment yet that the letters have intent, not accident. The fog is the day's primary physical correlative: the lake is hidden, not absent; the familiar is obscured rather than changed. Gerald's fifth notebook line, read back to himself twice, marks an escalation in his own awareness — he is no longer just recording, he is listening to what he has written. Doreen counting the chairs after is the quietly devastating beat she would never admit to. The undercurrent rises not from any single event but from the accumulation: four letters, three existing addresses and one new one, fog that cuts visibility to forty feet at the exact bend where you would see the water. The town is rationalising in real time. Wren's new lost-and-found policy, written at the bottom of the editor's note, is the clearest sign yet that the paper itself has accommodated the impossible into its administrative structure.

editor.name: Wren Aldercott
editor.voice: Dry, precise, allergic to drama, which is unfortunate given recent events. Writes like a person holding a clipboard while standing in rising water. Lets one true thing slip per edition, usually in the last line.

season_arc.premise: Something is using the Gazette's lost-and-found as a postal address — and the letters it sends back are addressed to people who have not yet arrived in Greywater Falls.
